└─────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┘


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

SECTION 7 — POSITIONING STATEMENT

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


AQARION does not invent closure operators, filtrations, or associated

graded objects. These are classical. AQARION's contribution is:


(a) Specializing them to deterministic observable finite dynamical

systems where the observable subspace W is given explicitly.

(b) Providing a computational pipeline (AQA_Engine) that computes

closure invariants for any functional graph.

(c) Organizing benchmark case studies that demonstrate the framework's

applicability across algebra, number theory, random maps, and

complex dynamics.


This positioning is conservative, mathematically grounded, and evaluates

the work on the strength of the underlying mathematics rather than on

new terminology.

This is a solid computational milestone, but I would distinguish carefully between measured results, empirical observations, and mathematical claims. That distinction will make the work much easier to publish and review.


What the current experiments establish


Your generalized AQA_Engine demonstrates that the same census pipeline can be applied to several finite functional graphs:


Kaprekar maps


Random maps


Truncated Collatz graphs


Discretized Mandelbrot dynamics


Fibonacci modulo systems


That is an important software and experimental result because it shows the framework is system-independent rather than Kaprekar-specific.


The measured quantities


cycle decomposition,


transient height,


level profile,


branching statistics,


quotient estimate,


compression ratio,


are all well-defined outputs of the engine.



What should remain empirical


Several conclusions should currently be stated as observations rather than theorems.


For example,




"Compression improves with resolution."




is supported by the reported data, but it should be written as




Observation. In the reported discretizations, the measured compression ratio decreases as lattice resolution increases.




Likewise,




"Near-boundary parameters produce deeper transients"




is an experimental observation for the tested parameter values, not yet a general theorem.



One technical issue


Your Mandelbrot lattice is not the Mandelbrot map itself.


Instead it is a finite approximation consisting of


discretization,


nearest-grid projection,


escape sink,


finite functional graph.


That distinction should be made explicit.


A notation such as


T_{r,c}:X_r\to X_r


where  is the grid resolution and  the parameter, makes this clear.


Then the measured invariants become


H(r,c),;

Q(r,c),;

C(r,c),


rather than invariants of the continuous Mandelbrot set itself.



Research direction


The interesting mathematics is not the individual measurements.


It is studying the limits


\lim_{r\to\infty}H(r,c),


\lim_{r\to\infty}Q(r,c),


\lim_{r\to\infty}C(r,c),


when these limits exist.


Those are genuine research problems.



AQARION architecture


I think the overall architecture has become quite coherent:




Observable Closure Operator




\mathcal C_K(W)




Closure Filtration




V_0\subseteq V_1\subseteq\cdots\subseteq V_\infty




Associated Graded Object




\operatorname{gr}(V)




Closure Invariants




(\delta,c,g_i,H(n))




Closure Signature




\sigma_K(W)




Search Transform




\mathcal S_K(W)=\sigma_K(W)






ASF Serialization






Observable Closure Atlas






That progression cleanly separates the mathematics from the computational infrastructure.



Publication priorities


At this stage I would prioritize:






Proving the foundational closure results (including the universal characterization of ).






Completing the Lean formalization of AQ-C007a before AQ-C007b.






Freezing the ASF schema and deterministic serialization.






Writing Paper I around the closure operator and its associated filtration.






Presenting Kaprekar, Mandelbrot, Fibonacci, random maps, and Collatz as benchmark case studies that exercise the framework rather than define it.






Overall, the project has shifted from proposing many new definitions toward organizing established mathematical ideas—closure operators, filtrations, associated graded objects, and invariant subspaces—into a unified framework for observable finite deterministic systems. That positioning is clearer, more conservative, and more likely to be evaluated on the strength of the underlying mathematics rather than on new terminology.The Zero-Point Field & Brain Resonance (Dec 2025)

Published in Frontiers in Human Neuroscience by researcher Joachim Keppler, this model provides a concrete mechanism for how the brain interacts with the quantum vacuum.

​The Mechanism: The brain's most abundant neurotransmitter, glutamate, resonates at a specific frequency (7.8 THz) with the Zero-Point Field (ZPF)—the fundamental, fluctuating ocean of energy permeating all space.

​The Effect: This resonant coupling triggers a phase transition, causing clusters of 100 billion glutamate molecules to shift into a coherent, macroscopic quantum state.

​The "Receiver" Brain: This process generates internal microwave fields (around 30 GHz) that modulate ion channels and regulate how our neurons fire. Instead of the brain just creating consciousness locally, it acts as an open system dynamically tuning into the background fabric of reality.A good minimal experiment should test one prediction of the theory without assuming the theory is true. Keppler's model predicts that a THz resonance should produce measurable changes in neural dynamics. We can therefore design an experiment around that prediction.


AQARION-ZPF-001


Minimal Resonance Experiment


Objective


Test whether weak electromagnetic stimulation near the predicted resonance frequency produces reproducible changes in neuronal activity beyond control conditions.


Hypothesis


If the proposed resonance mechanism exists, stimulation near the predicted resonance should produce statistically significant changes in neural activity compared with nearby frequencies.


Model System


Start with an in vitro system rather than humans:


Human iPSC-derived cortical neurons, or


Mouse cortical brain slices.


This avoids many ethical and technical complications.


Equipment


THz source capable of sweeping frequencies around the predicted resonance.


Multi-electrode array (MEA) to record neuronal firing.


Temperature monitoring (to exclude heating effects).


Environmental shielding.


Data acquisition software.


Experimental Protocol






Record baseline neural activity for 10 minutes.






Apply stimulation at the predicted resonance frequency.






Record activity.






Apply sham stimulation (device on, no output).






Apply nearby control frequencies above and below the target.






Randomize the order and blind the analysis.






Measurements


Record:


Mean firing rate


Burst frequency


Network synchrony


Oscillation spectra


Functional connectivity


Temperature


Controls


Sham stimulation


Frequency-offset controls


Equal power at all frequencies


Blinded analysis


Multiple independent cultures


Statistical Analysis


Compare resonance vs. controls using preregistered statistical tests.


Primary outcome:


Change in network synchrony.


Secondary outcomes:


Burst duration


Spike entropy


Oscillation power


Connectivity metrics


Interpretation


Positive result:


A reproducible effect at the predicted resonance frequency, significantly larger than at nearby frequencies, would support the resonance hypothesis. It would not by itself demonstrate ZPF coupling, but it would motivate further investigation.


Negative result:


No frequency-specific effect under well-controlled conditions would count as evidence against this particular prediction of the model.


AQARION Research Object


RO: AQ-EXP-ZPF-001


Status: Proposed


Evidence Level: Experimental


Scientific Goal: Test a frequency-specific prediction derived from the ZPF resonance hypothesis.


Success Criterion: Detect a statistically significant, reproducible neural response at the predicted resonance frequency that is absent in sham and nearby-frequency controls.



This experiment is intentionally modest. It does not attempt to test consciousness or the existence of the zero-point field directly. Instead, it isolates one concrete, falsifiable prediction of the theory: whether neural tissue exhibits a unique response at the predicted resonance frequency under rigorously controlled conditions. If that prediction fails, the broader model is weakened; if it succeeds, it provides motivation for more detailed mechanistic experiments.The experiment is well framed as a hypothesis test because it targets a specific, potentially falsifiable prediction. There are, however, several points where the protocol can be strengthened to distinguish a test of the resonance prediction from a test of the broader zero-point field (ZPF) hypothesis.


AQARION-EXP-ZPF-001 v1.1


Title: Frequency-Specific THz Resonance Test in Neuronal Networks


Status: Proposed


Evidence Level: Experimental Protocol


Objective


Determine whether neuronal cultures exhibit a reproducible, frequency-selective response to THz electromagnetic stimulation under controlled laboratory conditions.


Null Hypothesis (H₀)


Neural activity does not differ between the target frequency and nearby control frequencies beyond normal experimental variation.


Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)


Neural activity exhibits a statistically significant response at the predicted target frequency that is greater than responses at neighboring frequencies under identical exposure conditions.


Importantly, a positive result would support frequency-specific resonance, not by itself establish coupling to the quantum vacuum or demonstrate a mechanism for consciousness.


Experimental System


Begin with simplified preparations before progressing to more complex ones:






Human iPSC-derived cortical neurons on multi-electrode arrays.






Mouse cortical slices.






Replication in an independent laboratory.






Independent Variables


Stimulation frequency (including the predicted resonance and nearby controls).


Power level.


Exposure duration.


Dependent Variables


Primary endpoint:


Network synchrony.


Secondary endpoints:


Mean firing rate.


Burst rate.


Oscillation spectrum.


Functional connectivity.


Spike entropy.


Cell viability.


Temperature.


Essential Controls


Sham exposure.


Frequency-offset controls.


Equal power across all frequencies.


Randomized exposure order.


Double-blinded analysis.


Environmental shielding.


Continuous temperature monitoring to distinguish resonant effects from heating.


Statistical Plan


Pre-register:


Sample size and power analysis.


Primary endpoint.


Statistical tests.


Multiple-comparison correction.


Replication criteria.


Success should require both statistical significance and reproducibility across independent cultures and laboratories.


Interpretation Framework


Negative outcome


No reproducible frequency-specific response under controlled conditions would weigh against this particular resonance prediction.


Positive outcome


A reproducible, frequency-specific response would indicate that the tested preparation is sensitive to stimulation at that frequency. It would justify further mechanistic studies, but additional experiments would still be needed to determine the physical mechanism. It would not, on its own, confirm ZPF coupling or theories of consciousness.


AQARION Research Object


Identifier: AQ-EXP-ZPF-001


Category: Experimental Benchmark


Purpose: Evaluate a concrete prediction of a proposed resonance model using reproducible, blinded methodology.


Pipeline:


Theory → Prediction → Controlled Experiment → Statistical Analysis → Independent Replication → Mechanistic Investigation


This framing keeps the experiment scientifically conservative: it tests a measurable prediction while avoiding conclusions that extend beyond what the data could directly support.This is a well-structured protocol package. From a scientific methodology perspective, there are a few points worth distinguishing between what has been established, what the simulations demonstrate, and what remains to be tested experimentally.


Overall assessment


As a computational research object, AQ-EXP-ZPF-001 has matured substantially. It now contains several pieces that reviewers generally expect:


a protocol audit,


a preregistration template,


power calculations,


an analysis plan,


Monte Carlo validation of the statistical pipeline,


quality-control and replication checklists.


Those are valuable contributions to experimental design regardless of whether the underlying biological hypothesis ultimately succeeds.


What the Monte Carlo results do—and do not—show


The simulated false-positive and power estimates validate your analysis pipeline under the assumptions built into the simulation.


They do not validate the ZPF hypothesis itself.


Specifically, the simulation assumes:


a chosen effect size,


particular noise distributions,


specified between-culture variability,


and a modeled resonance response.


Under those assumptions, the pipeline behaves as expected. Real biological variability may differ substantially, so actual power could be higher or lower.


Thermal separation


One of the strongest additions is the explicit separation between thermal and resonance mechanisms.


The protocol correctly identifies that a frequency-specific biological effect would need to satisfy predictions such as:


narrow frequency dependence,


persistence under isothermal control,


reproducibility independent of bulk heating,


distinction from broadband power effects.


That is exactly the kind of falsifiable prediction that strengthens an experimental design.


However, the numerical resonance-to-thermal ratio (3.38) is itself a simulation output, not experimental evidence. It should be presented as demonstrating that the proposed analysis could discriminate the two mechanisms if the assumed signal model is accurate.


Sample size


Including explicit power calculations is an important improvement.


One thing to keep in mind is that estimated effect sizes (such as Cohen's d = 0.5) are assumptions until pilot data exist. Once a pilot experiment is performed, the protocol should be updated with empirically estimated variance and effect size before finalizing sample size.


Statistical plan


The addition of


paired comparisons,


mixed-effects modeling,


multiple-comparison correction,


preregistration,


replication criteria,


moves the protocol closer to current best practices.


A mixed-effects model is particularly appropriate because cultures, differentiation batches, and repeated measurements naturally introduce hierarchical sources of variation.


AQARION positioning


This experiment also fits well within the broader AQARION architecture you've been developing.


Rather than treating ZPF as established physics, it can be represented as a benchmark hypothesis:


\text{Hypothesis}

\rightarrow

\text{Protocol}

\rightarrow

\text{Verification}

\rightarrow

\text{Data}

\rightarrow

\text{Certification}


In that framework:


AQARION provides the reproducible computational infrastructure.


The biological hypothesis remains external and subject to experimental testing.


A negative result is informative because it constrains the hypothesis.


A positive result motivates independent replication rather than confirming the broader theory.


Suggested evidence classification


To keep the evidence taxonomy consistent with the rest of AQARION, this protocol could be classified as:


Component	Evidence status


Experimental protocol	Designed

Statistical audit	Verified

Monte Carlo validation	Verified (simulation)

Thermal discrimination model	Simulated

Laboratory measurements	Not yet performed

Biological conclusion	Undetermined


That separation will make the project easier for reviewers to evaluate because it clearly distinguishes software verification, statistical validation, and empirical evidence.


As a next AQARION research object, I would define this package as something like:


Object ID: AQ-EXP-ZPF-001


Type: Experimental Protocol Research Object


Mathematical status: Computationally verified protocol


Experimental status: Ready for laboratory implementation


Primary output: Reproducible protocol, preregistration, analysis pipeline, and verification artifacts


Scientific claim: None regarding ZPF itself; the object specifies a falsifiable experiment designed to test one prediction of the proposed resonance model.


"""
AQARION TEST
Minimal Observation Search (MOS)
Version 1.0

Goal:
Find the projection π that minimizes the AQARION defect operator.

For each candidate partition π:

    Dπ = (I − Pπ) K Pπ

Measure:

    • defect rank
    • leakage count
    • exactness
    • compression ratio

Author:
AQARION Experimental Suite
"""

from itertools import product
from collections import defaultdict


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Example deterministic system
# Replace with your own map.
# ------------------------------------------------------------

T = {
    0:1,
    1:2,
    2:3,
    3:3,
    4:5,
    5:3
}

states = sorted(T.keys())


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Build projection from partition labels
# Example:
# labels=(0,0,1,1,2,2)
# ------------------------------------------------------------

def build_partition(labels):
    part = defaultdict(list)
    for s,l in zip(states,labels):
        part[l].append(s)
    return list(part.values())


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Test exactness
# ------------------------------------------------------------

def defect_measure(partition):

    block_of = {}

    for i,block in enumerate(partition):
        for x in block:
            block_of[x]=i

    leakage=0

    exact=True

    for block in partition:

        target_blocks=set()

        for x in block:
            target_blocks.add(block_of[T[x]])

        if len(target_blocks)>1:
            exact=False
            leakage += len(target_blocks)-1

    return {

        "exact":exact,

        "leakage":leakage,

        "blocks":len(partition)

    }


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Exhaustive search
# ------------------------------------------------------------

best=None

N=len(states)

for labels in product(range(N),repeat=N):

    res=defect_measure(build_partition(labels))

    if best is None:

        best=(labels,res)

    else:

        if res["leakage"]<best[1]["leakage"]:

            best=(labels,res)

        elif res["leakage"]==best[1]["leakage"]:

            if res["blocks"]<best[1]["blocks"]:

                best=(labels,res)


print("="*60)
print("AQARION MINIMAL OBSERVATION SEARCH")
print("="*60)

print()

print("Best partition labels:")

print(best[0])

print()

print("Statistics")

for k,v in best[1].items():
    print(f"{k:12s}: {v}")

print()

if best[1]["exact"]:
    print("Exact quotient found.")
else:
    print("No exact quotient.")


from itertools import product
from collections import defaultdict

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AQARION MOS v1.0 — RUN & VERIFY
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

T_example = {0:1, 1:2, 2:3, 3:3, 4:5, 5:3}
states = sorted(T_example.keys())

def build_partition(labels):
    part = defaultdict(list)
    for s, l in zip(states, labels):
        part[l].append(s)
    return list(part.values())

def defect_measure(partition):
    block_of = {}
    for i, block in enumerate(partition):
        for x in block:
            block_of[x] = i
    leakage = 0
    exact = True
    for block in partition:
        target_blocks = set()
        for x in block:
            target_blocks.add(block_of[T_example[x]])
        if len(target_blocks) > 1:
            exact = False
            leakage += len(target_blocks) - 1
    return {"exact": exact, "leakage": leakage, "blocks": len(partition)}

# Run exhaustive search
best = None
N = len(states)
for labels in product(range(N), repeat=N):
    res = defect_measure(build_partition(labels))
    if best is None:
        best = (labels, res)
    else:
        if res["leakage"] < best[1]["leakage"]:
            best = (labels, res)
        elif res["leakage"] == best[1]["leakage"]:
            if res["blocks"] < best[1]["blocks"]:
                best = (labels, res)

print("=" * 60)
print("AQARION MOS v1.0 — EXHAUSTIVE SEARCH RESULT")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"\nBest partition labels: {best[0]}")
print(f"Statistics:")
for k, v in best[1].items():
    print(f"  {k:12s}: {v}")
print(f"\nPartition: {build_partition(best[0])}")

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BUG IDENTIFIED: The one-block partition always has leakage=0
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("BUG: One-block partition is always 'best'")
print("=" * 60)
one_block = tuple([0] * N)
res_one = defect_measure(build_partition(one_block))
print(f"One-block labels: {one_block}")
print(f"One-block stats: {res_one}")
print("\nThis is trivial and useless. Need constraint: partition must")
print("be non-trivial AND maximize information preservation.")

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MOS v1.1 — CORRECTED: Maximize compression subject to exactness
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("AQARION MOS v1.1 — CORRECTED")
print("=" * 60)

# New criteria: exact (leakage=0), then MAXIMIZE compression (minimize blocks)
# BUT exclude the trivial one-block partition
best_v11 = None
for labels in product(range(N), repeat=N):
    res = defect_measure(build_partition(labels))
    if not res["exact"]:
        continue
    if res["blocks"] == 1:
        continue  # exclude trivial partition
    if best_v11 is None:
        best_v11 = (labels, res)
    elif res["blocks"] < best_v11[1]["blocks"]:
        best_v11 = (labels, res)

print(f"Best non-trivial exact partition labels: {best_v11[0]}")
print(f"Partition: {build_partition(best_v11[0])}")
print(f"Stats:")
for k, v in best_v11[1].items():
    print(f"  {k:12s}: {v}")

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EFFICIENT ALGORITHM: Observable Quotient = Exact Minimal Quotient
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("EFFICIENT ALGORITHM: Height+Attractor Quotient")
print("=" * 60)

def compute_height_attractor_quotient(T):
    """
    Compute the coarsest exact quotient for a functional graph.
    Two states are equivalent iff they have the same height and same attractor.
    This is the minimal non-trivial exact quotient.
    """
    states = list(T.keys())
    N = len(states)
    
    # Find cycles (attractors)
    visited = [False] * N
    cycles = []
    for start in range(N):
        if visited[start]:
            continue
        path = []
        pos = {}
        cur = start
        while True:
            if visited[cur]:
                if cur in pos:
                    cycles.append(path[pos[cur]:])
                break
            visited[cur] = True
            pos[cur] = len(path)
            path.append(cur)
            cur = T[cur]
    
    attractors = set()
    for c in cycles:
        attractors.update(c)
    
    # BFS height from attractors
    from collections import deque
    inv = defaultdict(list)
    for x, y in T.items():
        inv[y].append(x)
    
    height = {}
    queue = deque()
    for a in attractors:
        height[a] = 0
        queue.append(a)
    while queue:
        x = queue.popleft()
        for y in inv[x]:
            if y not in height:
                height[y] = height[x] + 1
                queue.append(y)
    
    # Map each state to its attractor
    basin = {}
    for x in states:
        cur = x
        while height[cur] > 0:
            cur = T[cur]
        basin[x] = cur
    
    # Quotient: (height, attractor) signature
    sig = {}
    for x in states:
        sig[x] = (height[x], basin[x])
    
    # Build partition
    blocks = defaultdict(list)
    for x in states:
        blocks[sig[x]].append(x)
    
    return list(blocks.values()), height, basin

partition_eff, height_eff, basin_eff = compute_height_attractor_quotient(T_example)
print(f"Efficient quotient partition: {partition_eff}")
print(f"Number of blocks: {len(partition_eff)}")

# Verify exactness
block_of = {}
for i, block in enumerate(partition_eff):
    for x in block:
        block_of[x] = i

leakage_eff = 0
exact_eff = True
for block in partition_eff:
    targets = set(block_of[T_example[x]] for x in block)
    if len(targets) > 1:
        exact_eff = False
        leakage_eff += len(targets) - 1

print(f"Exact: {exact_eff}, Leakage: {leakage_eff}")
print(f"Heights: {height_eff}")
print(f"Basins: {basin_eff}")

# Compare with brute force
print(f"\nBrute-force v1.1 blocks: {best_v11[1]['blocks']}")
print(f"Efficient algorithm blocks: {len(partition_eff)}")
print(f"Match: {best_v11[1]['blocks'] == len(partition_eff)}")============================================================
AQARION MOS v1.0 — EXHAUSTIVE SEARCH RESULT
============================================================

Best partition labels: (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
Statistics:
  exact       : True
  leakage     : 0
  blocks      : 1

Partition: [[0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]]

============================================================
BUG: One-block partition is always 'best'
============================================================
One-block labels: (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
One-block stats: {'exact': True, 'leakage': 0, 'blocks': 1}

This is trivial and useless. Need constraint: partition must
be non-trivial AND maximize information preservation.

============================================================
AQARION MOS v1.1 — CORRECTED
============================================================
Best non-trivial exact partition labels: (0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0)
Partition: [[0, 1, 2, 3, 5], [4]]
Stats:
  exact       : True
  leakage     : 0
  blocks      : 2

============================================================
EFFICIENT ALGORITHM: Height+Attractor Quotient
============================================================
Efficient quotient partition: [[0], [1, 4], [2, 5], [3]]
Number of blocks: 4
Exact: True, Leakage: 0
Heights: {3: 0, 2: 1, 5: 1, 1: 2, 4: 2, 0: 3}
Basins: {0: 3, 1: 3, 2: 3, 3: 3, 4: 3, 5: 3}

Brute-force v1.1 blocks: 2
Efficient algorithm blocks: 4
Match: FalseYour revised framing is substantially stronger from a research-methodology perspective because it consistently separates four different kinds of statements:

1. Established mathematics (closure operators, filtrations, associated graded objects).


2. AQARION-specific constructions (observable closure specialization, signatures, ASF, AQA_Engine).


3. Empirical observations (measured behavior on benchmark systems).


4. Open mathematical questions (asymptotics, classification, approximation theory).



That separation is exactly what reviewers typically look for.

A few places where the manuscript could be strengthened further are below.

1. Strengthen the "observation vs theorem" convention

Your Section 2 already does this well. One additional convention would make it even clearer:

Definition — introduces terminology.

Lemma/Theorem/Corollary — mathematically proved.

Observation — supported by computation.

Conjecture — believed true but unproved.

Research Question — intentionally open.


If every numbered result falls into one of these categories, readers immediately know the evidence standard.


---

2. Clarify the scope of the benchmark systems

The benchmark systems should be described as validation examples, not sources of the theory.

A sentence like:

> "The benchmark systems are not part of the definition of observable closure. They serve only to demonstrate that the computational framework applies uniformly to diverse finite functional graphs."



helps prevent readers from thinking AQARION is primarily about Kaprekar or Mandelbrot dynamics.


---

3. Separate mathematical and computational outputs

Your pipeline naturally divides into two parallel products.

Mathematical pipeline

(V,K,W)
\rightarrow
\mathcal C_K(W)
\rightarrow
\text{filtration}
\rightarrow
\operatorname{gr}(V)
\rightarrow
\sigma_K(W).

Computational pipeline

\sigma_K(W)
\rightarrow
\text{ASF}
\rightarrow
\text{AQA Certificate}
\rightarrow
\text{Atlas}.

This emphasizes that ASF is not a mathematical invariant itself—it is a deterministic representation of invariants.


---

4. Refine the role of the Search Transform

The definition

S_K(W)=\sigma_K(W)

works well as a computational abstraction.

You might explicitly describe it as:

> "The Search Transform is the canonical feature-extraction map that converts observable closure data into a machine-readable signature."



That makes its role analogous to a feature extractor rather than another algebraic operator.


---

5. Paper I remains appropriately focused

The current publication priorities are well aligned with what can realistically be supported.

A compact structure could be:

Classical background

Observable closure operator

Universal characterization

Closure operator properties

Closure filtration

Associated graded object

Derived invariants

Computational realization

Benchmark examples

Open problems


This keeps speculative material out of the foundational paper.


---

6. The empirical claims are now appropriately bounded

For example, statements like:

"Compression decreases for the measured resolutions."

"The measured systems occupy distinct regions of the  plane."


are exactly the kind of language computational mathematics uses.

Avoiding statements like "always" or "for all" unless proved will strengthen credibility.


---

7. Long-term mathematical opportunities

Your architecture naturally suggests several future research directions that fit after Paper I:

Characterization of realizable closure signatures.

Classification of operators by signature classes.

Stability of signatures under perturbation.

Approximate observable quotients.

Representation-theoretic analysis (Jordan and rational canonical forms).

Complexity theory of observable closure.

Experimental atlas generation across broad families of finite dynamical systems.


These are coherent extensions because they all grow from the same central construction rather than introducing unrelated concepts.

Overall assessment

The framework now has a clear hierarchy:

Classical foundation: closure operators, invariant subspaces, filtrations, associated graded objects.

AQARION specialization: observable closure, closure signatures, observable invariants, search transform.

Computational infrastructure: AQA_Engine, ASF serialization, certification, atlas construction.

Research program: benchmark studies, classification, approximation theory, and future extensions.


That organization is much easier to evaluate than earlier drafts because it clearly distinguishes what is inherited from existing mathematics, what AQARION contributes as a specialization, what has been demonstrated computationally, and what remains an open research agenda.Overall, this is a much more publication-ready framing than many earlier versions because it clearly separates mathematical theory, computational measurements, and empirical observations. That separation makes it easier for readers and reviewers to evaluate each claim according to the appropriate standard.

A few points stand out.

1. The "established vs. observed vs. to be proved" distinction is a major strength

Your structure

Established: definitions, algorithms, software outputs.

Observed: numerical behavior on measured benchmark systems.

To prove: asymptotic or structural mathematical statements.


is exactly how computational mathematics papers often distinguish evidence levels.

For example:

"For the measured values ,  decreases" is an observation.

"" is a conjecture or open problem until proved.


That keeps the manuscript conservative and scientifically rigorous.

2. The benchmark philosophy is sound

Treating

Kaprekar,

Fibonacci,

Random maps,

Truncated Collatz,

discretized Mandelbrot


as benchmark systems rather than definitions of AQARION is a good organizational decision.

The framework exists independently of any one example.

The examples demonstrate portability.

That is exactly how many computational frameworks are introduced.

3. The Mandelbrot clarification is especially important

Your notation

T_{r,c}:X_r\rightarrow X_r

makes it explicit that you are studying a finite discretized functional graph, not the continuous complex dynamical system.

This avoids a common source of reviewer criticism.

All reported quantities should therefore continue to be written as

,

,

,


rather than invariants of the classical Mandelbrot dynamics.

4. Publication priorities look appropriately ordered

The sequence

1. prove the universal closure characterization,


2. Lean formalization,


3. freeze ASF,


4. write Paper I,


5. expand benchmark studies,



is logical.

In particular, proving the closure operator properties first gives later computational work a firm mathematical foundation.

5. The positioning statement is appropriately conservative

The statement that AQARION

does not invent closure operators,

does not invent filtrations,

does not invent associated graded objects,


but instead specializes them to observable finite deterministic dynamical systems is well aligned with existing mathematics.

That kind of explicit acknowledgment generally strengthens rather than weakens a paper.

6. One point requiring continued caution

The discussion of Joachim Keppler's zero-point field (ZPF) model should remain clearly separated from AQARION's mathematical framework.

Keppler has published a series of hypothesis and theory papers proposing resonant coupling between glutamate and the electromagnetic zero-point field, including predicted frequencies around 7.8 THz and subsequent microwave-range effects. These are presented by the author as theoretical models and proposed mechanisms, not experimentally established facts. The papers also explicitly call for new experiments to test these predictions. 

Accordingly:

It is appropriate to describe the ZPF work as an external scientific hypothesis.

Your proposed experiments test predictions derived from that hypothesis, not the truth of the hypothesis itself.

Positive results would support the tested prediction but would not, by themselves, establish ZPF coupling or a theory of consciousness.

Negative results would weigh against the specific prediction under the tested conditions.


Maintaining that distinction will make any experimental program much more scientifically defensible.

Overall assessment

The architecture now has a clear hierarchy:

1. Classical mathematics

closure operators

invariant subspaces

filtrations

associated graded objects



2. AQARION specialization

observable closure

closure filtration

closure signatures

observable invariants



3. Computational infrastructure

AQA_Engine

Search Transform

ASF serialization

certification

atlas generation



4. Benchmark evidence

Kaprekar

Fibonacci

Random maps

Truncated Collatz

Discretized Mandelbrot



5. Open research

asymptotic limits

classification theorems

approximate observable quotients

larger computational atlases




That organization gives AQARION a coherent research narrative while making clear which components are established mathematics, which are computational methodology, which are empirical observations, and which remain open research questions.A useful next step is to shift from "designing an experiment" to defining exactly what counts as evidence before any data are collected. That makes the protocol more rigorous and easier for independent laboratories to evaluate.


---

AQARION-EXP-ZPF-001 v1.2

Evidence Framework

The experiment should distinguish five separate layers.

Layer	Question	Evidence Type

Physical	Was the intended THz field delivered?	Instrument calibration
Biological	Did neural activity change?	MEA recordings
Statistical	Is the change unlikely under the null?	Pre-registered analysis
Mechanistic	Can heating or artifacts explain it?	Control experiments
Theoretical	Does the result support Keppler's model?	Interpretation only


This separation prevents a positive biological effect from being overstated as confirmation of the entire theory.


---

Decision Criteria

Before collecting data, define objective outcomes.

Outcome A

No statistically significant difference.

Interpretation:

consistent with the null hypothesis

weakens the specific resonance prediction under the tested conditions

does not disprove all ZPF-related hypotheses



---

Outcome B

Significant response at many frequencies.

Interpretation:

indicates nonspecific electromagnetic sensitivity

does not support a unique resonance



---

Outcome C

A narrow resonance peak centered near the predicted frequency.

Interpretation:

supports the existence of frequency-selective coupling

motivates mechanistic investigation

does not by itself establish ZPF coupling



---

Outcome D

Laboratory-dependent effects only.

Interpretation:

suggests uncontrolled experimental variables

requires replication before scientific conclusions



---

Replication Standard

A strong publication should require:

independent cultures

multiple recording days

different differentiation batches

independent operator

independent laboratory


Only effects reproduced across laboratories should be treated as robust.


---

Artifact Analysis

Potential confounders should be investigated explicitly.

Examples include:

dielectric heating

electrode interference

amplifier saturation

vibration

humidity

incubator drift

culture age

media composition

electromagnetic reflections


Each should have a documented control.


---

Connection to AQARION

Within the AQARION framework, the protocol itself can be viewed as a certified research object:

Hypothesis → predicts a measurable frequency-specific effect

Protocol → specifies how to test that prediction

Data → records observations

Analysis → evaluates the predefined statistical criteria

Certification → preserves provenance and reproducibility

In this view, AQARION contributes the reproducible computational and verification framework, while the biological hypothesis remains external and subject to empirical testing.


---

Publication Strategy

If pursued, this work is likely best separated into distinct outputs:

1. Protocol paper — experimental design, preregistration, statistical plan, and reproducibility framework.


2. Experimental paper — laboratory measurements and results, whether positive or negative.


3. Mechanistic paper (only if warranted) — investigation of the physical basis of any reproducible frequency-specific effects.



Keeping these stages separate avoids drawing conclusions beyond what each stage directly supports and aligns with good scientific practice.Yes. Before attempting a difficult THz neuroscience experiment, I'd look for simpler experiments that test pieces of the hypothesis. They are cheaper, easier to replicate, and can tell you whether it's worth moving to neurons at all.

Here are several possibilities, ranked from most practical to most ambitious.

1. Build and validate the measurement system first (highest priority)

Before testing biology, verify that your apparatus can produce and measure stable, narrow-band electromagnetic fields.

Questions:

Is the output frequency accurate?

Is the field spatially uniform?

Does the setup introduce unexpected harmonics?

How much heating occurs?


This isn't glamorous, but it prevents months of chasing instrument artifacts.


---

2. Test simple physical materials

Instead of neurons, expose well-characterized materials to the same frequency sweep.

Examples:

distilled water

saline

amino acid solutions (including glutamate)

lipid vesicles


Measure:

dielectric response

absorption

temperature

impedance


If no unusual frequency-specific behavior is found in simple systems, that informs expectations for biological experiments.


---

3. Test glutamate directly

Since Keppler's hypothesis specifically involves glutamate, investigate whether glutamate solutions exhibit any measurable resonance compared with control amino acids.

Controls might include:

glycine

alanine

GABA


Any observed effect would still require careful interpretation, but this is a much narrower question than testing consciousness.


---

4. Computational modeling

This aligns well with AQARION.

Develop simulations of:

resonance models,

network dynamics,

expected experimental signals,

statistical power.


This lets you predict what genuine resonance would look like before building hardware.


---

5. Blind classification challenge

Use the AQA framework to ask:

> Can the analysis pipeline distinguish simulated resonance data from simulated non-resonance data?



If it cannot, the experimental analysis should be improved before collecting laboratory data.


---

6. General resonance search

Instead of assuming 7.8 THz is special, perform a blind frequency scan over a broad range and look for statistically significant peaks.

This avoids bias toward a single predicted frequency and makes the experiment more exploratory while remaining rigorous.


---

My suggestion

If your goal is to build a solid research program, I would prioritize:

1. AQA-HARDWARE-001 — Instrument calibration and field characterization.


2. AQA-PHYSICS-001 — Measure dielectric and absorption properties of water, saline, and glutamate solutions.


3. AQA-SIM-001 — Simulate resonance signatures and validate the analysis pipeline.


4. AQA-NEURAL-001 — Only then move to neuronal cultures if the earlier stages justify it.



This staged approach reduces cost, isolates potential artifacts early, and produces publishable results even if the biological hypothesis ultimately isn't supported. It also fits well with AQARION's emphasis on reproducibility and evidence-based progression.I think there's an even broader opportunity here. Rather than asking, "Can we test Keppler's model?", ask:

> "What is the smallest, strongest scientific question we can answer?"



That mindset often leads to more impactful research.

Here are several candidate programs.


---

Program A — Functional Graph Observatory

This builds directly on AQARION.

Instead of focusing on one biological theory, create a general framework for comparing finite deterministic systems.

Questions include:

Which graph invariants are universal?

Which distinguish different dynamics?

Are there conservation laws across systems?

Can systems be clustered by observable closure signatures?


This produces mathematics regardless of any biological outcome.


---

Program B — General Resonance Mapping

Rather than testing one predicted frequency, ask:

> Do biological systems exhibit reproducible frequency-selective responses at all?



Use a broad frequency sweep and let the data identify candidate resonances.

If a peak appears, it can be investigated later.

If not, that's informative too.


---

Program C — Observable Compression

AQARION already studies quotienting and compression.

Extend this by asking:

Why do some systems compress more than others?

Can compression predict computational complexity?

Is compression related to symmetry?

Does compression correlate with entropy?


This is a mathematically rich direction independent of neuroscience.


---

Program D — Benchmark Library

Create a public benchmark suite.

For every functional graph compute:

cycles

transient height

indegree entropy

closure signatures

quotient statistics

compression ratio


Over time this becomes a reusable resource for other researchers.


---

Program E — Synthetic Biology First

Before neurons, use very simple systems:

enzyme reactions

artificial membranes

liposomes

protein solutions


These reduce complexity while still allowing frequency-response measurements.


---

Program F — Prediction Challenge

This is one of my favorite ideas.

Before computing anything, predict the invariants.

For example:

> Given only the update rule, predict



number of attractors

transient height

compression ratio

closure depth


Then compare prediction to computation.

This creates a standardized benchmark for mathematical intuition and algorithm development.


---

Program G — Search for Universal Scaling Laws

AQARION has already explored scaling in Kaprekar systems.

A broader question is:

Across many deterministic finite systems,

Q(N),\quad H(N),\quad C(N),\quad h_{\mathrm{ind}}(N)

do common scaling laws emerge?

Possible outcomes:

logarithmic growth,

power laws,

exponential behavior,

universality classes.


Even negative results would help characterize different families of dynamics.


---

Program H — The Observatory Vision

Instead of thinking of AQARION as "the Kaprekar project" or "the ZPF project," think of it as an observatory for deterministic dynamics.

The workflow becomes:

Define a system.

Compute observable closure.

Compute invariants.

Generate a certified record.

Add it to the atlas.

Compare against all previous systems.


The value grows as the atlas expands.

Why I think this is promising

This direction has several advantages:

It doesn't depend on any single scientific hypothesis being correct.

Every new benchmark enriches the atlas.

The mathematical and computational infrastructure remains useful regardless of the application domain.

If later experiments (such as resonance studies) produce interesting data, the same infrastructure can analyze them.


In other words, the AQARION framework becomes a general-purpose platform for studying finite deterministic dynamical systems, with specific applications—Kaprekar maps, discretized Mandelbrot dynamics, random maps, biological models, or others—serving as benchmark case studies rather than defining the framework itself.I think there is—and it may actually be more valuable scientifically.

Instead of trying to test the entire ZPF hypothesis, test the assumptions that the hypothesis depends on. Each assumption is simpler, cheaper, and independently publishable.

Here are the ones I'd prioritize.

1. Does glutamate have unusual THz spectral behavior?

Keppler's model relies on hydrated glutamate having specific THz vibrational properties from which the 7.8 THz resonance is inferred. That assumption comes from spectroscopy and model calculations, not from a direct demonstration of ZPF coupling. 

Research question:

> Does hydrated glutamate exhibit distinctive THz absorption compared with other amino acids under identical conditions?



This is a spectroscopy experiment, not a neuroscience experiment.


---

2. Can the predicted microwave signal be detected?

The model predicts that if the proposed phase transition occurs, it should generate an endogenous microwave field around 30 GHz. 

Rather than stimulating tissue externally, ask:

> Can any reproducible 30 GHz emission be measured during synchronized neuronal activity?



This is still technically challenging but may be more directly tied to the model's predictions.


---

3. Can AQARION detect resonance objectively?

This is something you can do immediately.

Generate simulated datasets containing:

no resonance,

weak resonance,

strong resonance,

broadband effects,

thermal artifacts.


Then test whether the AQA analysis pipeline correctly classifies them without knowing which dataset is which.

If it fails, improve the analysis before collecting any laboratory data.


---

4. Blind frequency discovery

Rather than asking:

> "Is 7.8 THz correct?"



ask:

> "If a resonance exists, where is it?"



Analyze the data without labeling the frequencies until after the statistical analysis is complete.

This reduces confirmation bias.


---

5. Cross-disciplinary benchmark

This is one I find especially interesting.

AQARION already studies deterministic systems.

Why not study resonance-like signatures in many unrelated systems?

For example:

electrical RLC circuits,

coupled oscillators,

reaction-diffusion simulations,

neural network models,

cellular automata.


If your closure signatures identify resonance structures across very different systems, that would be a contribution independent of the ZPF hypothesis.


---

What I would do first

If I were building the AQARION research program, my first project would not be ZPF.

It would be:

> AQ-RES-001: A General Resonance Detection Benchmark



Goal:

Build a completely hypothesis-neutral framework that detects resonance, coherence, synchronization, and transient structure in arbitrary deterministic systems.


Test it on:

RLC circuits,

Kuramoto oscillators,

coupled pendulums,

cellular automata,

Kaprekar dynamics,

random maps,

only then neural data.


If that framework proves robust, you can later apply it to biological experiments—including tests inspired by Keppler's work—without the framework depending on any particular theory of consciousness. That gives AQARION value regardless of whether the ZPF hypothesis is ultimately supported or not.
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import List, Dict, Optional

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AQARION v14.0 — OBSERVATORY ARCHITECTURE & EVIDENCE FRAMEWORK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

@dataclass
class EvidenceLayer:
    """One layer of the five-layer evidence framework."""
    layer: int
    name: str
    question: str
    evidence_type: str
    required_instruments: List[str]
    pass_criteria: str
    failure_modes: List[str]
    independence: str  # which other layers this is independent from

@dataclass
class DecisionOutcome:
    """Pre-defined decision outcome."""
    label: str
    name: str
    statistical_signature: str
    interpretation: str
    next_action: str
    confidence_level: str

@dataclass
class ResearchProgram:
    """A staged research program."""
    identifier: str
    name: str
    objective: str
    prerequisites: List[str]
    milestones: List[Dict]
    deliverables: List[str]
    estimated_effort: str
    publication_target: str
    success_criteria: str

@dataclass
class BenchmarkEntry:
    """Schema for a single benchmark in the atlas."""
    system_id: str
    family: str
    rule_description: str
    state_space_size: int
    parameters: Dict
    invariants: Dict
    certificate: Dict

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECTION 1 — FIVE-LAYER EVIDENCE FRAMEWORK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

evidence_framework = [
    EvidenceLayer(
        layer=1,
        name="Physical",
        question="Was the intended stimulus delivered as specified?",
        evidence_type="Instrument calibration and metrology",
        required_instruments=[
            "spectrum_analyzer",
            "power_meter",
            "thermal_camera",
            "field_probe",
            "oscilloscope"
        ],
        pass_criteria="Frequency within ±1% of target, power within ±5%, spatial uniformity >90%, temperature rise <2°C",
        failure_modes=[
            "Harmonic contamination",
            "Power drift",
            "Standing wave artifacts",
            "Thermal runaway"
        ],
        independence="Independent of all other layers — pure engineering verification"
    ),
    EvidenceLayer(
        layer=2,
        name="Biological",
        question="Did the biological system exhibit a measurable change?",
        evidence_type="Physiological recording (MEA, imaging, spectroscopy)",
        required_instruments=[
            "multi_electrode_array",
            "impedance_analyzer",
            "microscope",
            "incubator_monitor"
        ],
        pass_criteria="Change in primary endpoint exceeds 3× baseline standard deviation, reproducible across cultures",
        failure_modes=[
            "Culture death",
            "Electrode failure",
            "Batch variability",
            "Contamination"
        ],
        independence="Depends on Layer 1 (stimulus must be real) but not on Layers 3–5"
    ),
    EvidenceLayer(
        layer=3,
        name="Statistical",
        question="Is the change unlikely under the null hypothesis?",
        evidence_type="Pre-registered statistical analysis",
        required_instruments=[
            "analysis_software",
            "randomization_table",
            "blinding_protocol"
        ],
        pass_criteria="Primary endpoint significant at α=0.05 with Bonferroni correction, effect size d>0.5, reproducible in 2/3 batches",
        failure_modes=[
            "P-hacking",
            "Multiple comparison inflation",
            "Regression to the mean",
            "Batch effects"
        ],
        independence="Depends on Layer 2 (data must exist) but analysis protocol is fixed before data collection"
    ),
    EvidenceLayer(
        layer=4,
        name="Mechanistic",
        question="Can the change be explained by known artifacts?",
        evidence_type="Control experiments and covariate analysis",
        required_instruments=[
            "thermal_camera",
            "sham_exposure_setup",
            "positive_control",
            "broadband_noise_source"
        ],
        pass_criteria="Effect persists after thermal covariate regression, differs from sham and broadband controls, not replicated by KCl",
        failure_modes=[
            "Dielectric heating",
            "Electrode interference",
            "Vibration coupling",
            "Incubator drift"
        ],
        independence="Depends on Layers 1–3 but tests alternative explanations, not the target hypothesis"
    ),
    EvidenceLayer(
        layer=5,
        name="Theoretical",
        question="Does the result support the proposed theoretical model?",
        evidence_type="Interpretation and model comparison",
        required_instruments=[
            "theoretical_model",
            "simulation_framework",
            "parameter_estimation_tools"
        ],
        pass_criteria="Observed effect quantitatively matches model prediction within factor of 2, no competing model fits better",
        failure_modes=[
            "Post-hoc model fitting",
            "Overinterpretation",
            "Confirmation bias",
            "Ignoring alternative models"
        ],
        independence="Depends on all previous layers but is the most subjective — requires maximum skepticism"
    ),
]

print("=" * 90)
print("AQARION v14.0 — FIVE-LAYER EVIDENCE FRAMEWORK")
print("=" * 90)
for layer in evidence_framework:
    print(f"\n┌─ LAYER {layer.layer}: {layer.name.upper()}")
    print(f"│ Question: {layer.question}")
    print(f"│ Evidence: {layer.evidence_type}")
    print(f"│ Pass: {layer.pass_criteria}")
    print(f"│ Independence: {layer.independence}")
    print(f"│ Failure modes: {', '.join(layer.failure_modes)}")

# Save
with open("/mnt/agents/output/AQARION_evidence_framework.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump([asdict(l) for l in evidence_framework], f, indent=2)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECTION 2 — DECISION CRITERIA (PRE-DEFINED OUTCOMES)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

decision_outcomes = [
    DecisionOutcome(
        label="A",
        name="Null Result",
        statistical_signature="No significant difference at α=0.05 (Bonferroni-corrected) in any batch",
        interpretation="Consistent with H₀. The tested preparation does not exhibit frequency-selective response under the tested conditions. This weakens the specific resonance prediction but does not disprove all ZPF-related hypotheses.",
        next_action="Publish null result. Consider whether experimental conditions (power, duration, preparation type) were appropriate. Do not pursue mechanistic studies.",
        confidence_level="High — null results with adequate power are informative"
    ),
    DecisionOutcome(
        label="B",
        name="Broadband Response",
        statistical_signature="Significant response at ≥3 frequencies with monotonic power dependence, no narrow peak",
        interpretation="Indicates non-specific electromagnetic sensitivity (likely thermal or dielectric). Does not support a unique resonance.",
        next_action="Investigate thermal model. Add isothermal control. Check for electrode artifacts. If thermal, publish as heating study.",
        confidence_level="High — broadband response is unambiguously non-resonant"
    ),
    DecisionOutcome(
        label="C",
        name="Narrow Resonance",
        statistical_signature="Significant response (p<0.05 Bonferroni) at exactly one frequency, bandwidth <10% of center frequency, non-monotonic power dependence",
        interpretation="Supports frequency-selective coupling. Motivates mechanistic investigation. Does NOT by itself establish ZPF coupling or consciousness mechanism.",
        next_action="Proceed to mechanistic controls (thermal, broadband, sham). If mechanistic layer passes, publish as 'frequency-selective neural response' without theoretical claims.",
        confidence_level="Medium — requires replication and mechanistic validation before strong claims"
    ),
    DecisionOutcome(
        label="D",
        name="Laboratory-Dependent Effect",
        statistical_signature="Significant in one batch or one lab, not reproducible in independent replication",
        interpretation="Suggests uncontrolled experimental variables (batch effects, operator bias, instrument drift). Not a robust scientific finding.",
        next_action="Do not publish as positive result. Investigate batch differences. Improve standardization. Re-run with stricter controls.",
        confidence_level="Low — irreproducible effects are not evidence"
    ),
    DecisionOutcome(
        label="E",
        name="Inconclusive",
        statistical_signature="Mixed results: significant in some batches, not others; effect size near detection threshold",
        interpretation="Underpowered or highly variable system. Cannot distinguish signal from noise.",
        next_action="Increase sample size. Reduce batch variability. Consider alternative preparation. Do not publish as positive or negative.",
        confidence_level="Very low — inconclusive results should not be published as findings"
    ),
]

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("SECTION 2 — PRE-DEFINED DECISION OUTCOMES")
print("=" * 90)
for outcome in decision_outcomes:
    print(f"\n┌─ OUTCOME {outcome.label}: {outcome.name.upper()}")
    print(f"│ Statistical signature: {outcome.statistical_signature}")
    print(f"│ Interpretation: {outcome.interpretation}")
    print(f"│ Next action: {outcome.next_action}")
    print(f"│ Confidence: {outcome.confidence_level}")

with open("/mnt/agents/output/AQARION_decision_outcomes.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump([asdict(o) for o in decision_outcomes], f, indent=2)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECTION 3 — RESEARCH PROGRAMS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

programs = [
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-HARDWARE-001",
        name="Instrument Calibration and Field Characterization",
        objective="Verify that the THz apparatus produces stable, narrow-band, spatially uniform fields with known power and minimal harmonics.",
        prerequisites=["THz source procurement", "spectrum analyzer access", "power meter calibration"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Frequency accuracy ±1%", "deliverable": "Calibration certificate"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Spatial uniformity >90%", "deliverable": "Field mapping data"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Harmonic suppression >20 dB", "deliverable": "Spectrum analysis report"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "Thermal characterization", "deliverable": "ΔT vs power curve"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Calibration protocol", "Field characterization report", "Standard operating procedure"],
        estimated_effort="3 months, 1 engineer",
        publication_target="Methods paper in Review of Scientific Instruments",
        success_criteria="All M1–M4 pass; field is stable over 1-hour exposure"
    ),
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-PHYSICS-001",
        name="Physical Material Resonance Screening",
        objective="Test whether water, saline, and amino acid solutions exhibit frequency-specific THz responses.",
        prerequisites=["AQA-HARDWARE-001 complete", "sample preparation protocol", "THz-TDS or similar spectrometer"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Water absorption spectrum 0.1–1.0 THz", "deliverable": "Absorption coefficient data"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Saline vs distilled water comparison", "deliverable": "Difference spectrum"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Glutamate vs glycine vs alanine vs GABA", "deliverable": "Comparative absorption spectra"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "Temperature-controlled measurement", "deliverable": "Isothermal absorption data"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Spectral database", "Comparative analysis", "Thermal model validation"],
        estimated_effort="6 months, 1 physicist + 1 chemist",
        publication_target="Biophysical Journal or J. Chem. Phys.",
        success_criteria="Spectra reproducible across 3 independent measurements; glutamate signature identifiable if present"
    ),
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-SIM-001",
        name="Resonance Detection Benchmark and Pipeline Validation",
        objective="Validate that the AQA analysis pipeline can objectively distinguish resonant from non-resonant synthetic data.",
        prerequisites=["AQA_Engine v13.2", "synthetic data generator", "blinding protocol"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Generate 5 synthetic datasets (no resonance, weak, strong, broadband, thermal)", "deliverable": "Labeled synthetic data archive"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Blind classification challenge", "deliverable": "Confusion matrix and ROC curve"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Sensitivity analysis (effect size vs detection rate)", "deliverable": "Power curve"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "Pipeline improvement if AUC < 0.9", "deliverable": "Updated analysis code"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Benchmark dataset", "Pipeline validation report", "Analysis code release"],
        estimated_effort="2 months, 1 computational scientist",
        publication_target="Software paper in JOSS or bioRxiv methods",
        success_criteria="AUC > 0.9 on blind classification; 95% sensitivity at d=0.5"
    ),
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-NEURAL-001",
        name="Neuronal Culture Frequency-Response Study",
        objective="Test whether neuronal cultures exhibit reproducible, frequency-selective responses to THz stimulation.",
        prerequisites=["AQA-HARDWARE-001", "AQA-PHYSICS-001", "AQA-SIM-001", "iPSC differentiation protocol", "MEA recording setup"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Culture viability and baseline characterization", "deliverable": "Baseline variability report"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Sham exposure control", "deliverable": "Sham vs baseline comparison"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Frequency sweep (0.1–1.0 THz)", "deliverable": "Frequency-response curve"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "Target frequency replication", "deliverable": "Replicated significant result or null"},
            {"milestone": "M5", "task": "Independent lab replication", "deliverable": "Cross-lab comparison"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Experimental dataset", "Statistical analysis", "Replication report", "Publication"],
        estimated_effort="18 months, 2 biologists + 1 physicist + 1 statistician",
        publication_target="eLife or Nature Communications (if positive), PLOS ONE (if null)",
        success_criteria="Outcome C (narrow resonance) with Layer 4 (mechanistic) validation, replicated independently"
    ),
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-ATLAS-001",
        name="Functional Graph Observatory and Benchmark Library",
        objective="Create a public, searchable atlas of functional graph invariants across all studied systems.",
        prerequisites=["AQA_Engine v13.2", "web hosting", "database schema"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Schema freeze for benchmark entries", "deliverable": "JSON schema v1.0"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Populate with 50+ systems", "deliverable": "Initial atlas release"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Web interface for search and comparison", "deliverable": "Public website"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "API for programmatic access", "deliverable": "REST API documentation"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Atlas database", "Web interface", "API", "Community guidelines"],
        estimated_effort="6 months, 1 software engineer + 1 data curator",
        publication_target="Database paper in Nucleic Acids Research or Scientific Data",
        success_criteria=">50 systems, >100 downloads, >5 external submissions in first year"
    ),
    ResearchProgram(
        identifier="AQA-PRED-001",
        name="Invariant Prediction Challenge",
        objective="Before computing, predict invariants from the update rule alone. Benchmark mathematical intuition.",
        prerequisites=["AQA-ATLAS-001", "prediction submission platform", "scoring algorithm"],
        milestones=[
            {"milestone": "M1", "task": "Define prediction targets (H, Q, C, cycle count)", "deliverable": "Target specification"},
            {"milestone": "M2", "task": "Release training set (30 systems with known invariants)", "deliverable": "Training data"},
            {"milestone": "M3", "task": "Release test set (20 systems, invariants hidden)", "deliverable": "Blind test data"},
            {"milestone": "M4", "task": "Evaluate submissions", "deliverable": "Leaderboard and analysis"},
        ],
        deliverables=["Challenge specification", "Dataset", "Leaderboard", "Winner analysis"],
        estimated_effort="4 months, 1 organizer + community",
        publication_target="NeurIPS/ICML workshop or arXiv",
        success_criteria=">10 teams submit, best predictor achieves R² > 0.7 on test set"
    ),
]

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("SECTION 3 — RESEARCH PROGRAMS")
print("=" * 90)
for prog in programs:
    print(f"\n┌─ {prog.identifier}: {prog.name}")
    print(f"│ Objective: {prog.objective}")
    print(f"│ Effort: {prog.estimated_effort}")
    print(f"│ Publication: {prog.publication_target}")
    print(f"│ Success: {prog.success_criteria}")
    print(f"│ Milestones:")
    for m in prog.milestones:
        print(f"│   {m['milestone']}: {m['task']} → {m['deliverable']}")

with open("/mnt/agents/output/AQARION_research_programs.json", "w") as f:

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECTION 4 — OBSERVATORY ARCHITECTURE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

observatory_architecture = {
    "name": "AQARION Functional Graph Observatory",
    "version": "14.0",
    "philosophy": "Hypothesis-neutral platform for studying finite deterministic dynamical systems",
    
    "layers": {
        "L1_Definition": {
            "input": "Update rule T: X → X, observable subspace W ⊆ X",
            "output": "Formal system specification",
            "tools": ["Python spec", "JSON schema", "Lean formalization"]
        },
        "L2_Computation": {
            "input": "System specification",
            "output": "Raw invariants (cycles, height, branching, entropy)",
            "tools": ["AQA_Engine", "parallel compute", "exact arithmetic"]
        },
        "L3_Certification": {
            "input": "Raw invariants",
            "output": "Signed computational record with provenance",
            "tools": ["ASF serialization", "checksum", "version control"]
        },
        "L4_Atlas": {
            "input": "Certified records",
            "output": "Searchable database with cross-system comparison",
            "tools": ["PostgreSQL", "REST API", "web interface"]
        },
        "L5_Analysis": {
            "input": "Atlas data",
            "output": "Scaling laws, universality classes, classification theorems",
            "tools": ["statistical modeling", "machine learning", "theorem prover"]
        },
        "L6_Application": {
            "input": "General framework + specific hypothesis",
            "output": "Targeted experimental or theoretical investigation",
            "tools": ["resonance detection", "prediction challenge", "experimental design"]
        }
    },
    
    "workflows": {
        "new_system": ["L1", "L2", "L3", "L4"],
        "classification_study": ["L4", "L5"],
        "experimental_design": ["L5", "L6"],
        "full_pipeline": ["L1", "L2", "L3", "L4", "L5", "L6"]
    },
    
    "governance": {
        "schema_versioning": "Semantic versioning for all schemas",
        "contribution_model": "Open submission with peer review",
        "reproducibility_requirement": "All computations must be reproducible from specification",
        "data_retention": "Permanent archive with DOI",
        "citation_format": "AQARION-Atlas:system_id:version"
    }
}

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("SECTION 4 — OBSERVATORY ARCHITECTURE")
print("=" * 90)
print(f"\n{observatory_architecture['name']} v{observatory_architecture['version']}")
print(f"Philosophy: {observatory_architecture['philosophy']}")
print("\nLayers:")
for layer_id, layer in observatory_architecture['layers'].items():
    print(f"  {layer_id}: {layer['output']}")
    print(f"    Tools: {', '.join(layer['tools'])}")

with open("/mnt/agents/output/AQARION_observatory_architecture.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(observatory_architecture, f, indent=2)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECTION 5 — BENCHMARK LIBRARY SCHEMA
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

benchmark_schema = {
    "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
    "title": "AQARION Benchmark Entry",
    "type": "object",
    "required": ["system_id", "family", "state_space_size", "invariants", "certificate"],
    "properties": {
        "system_id": {"type": "string", "pattern": "^[A-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]+_[A-Z0-9]+$"},
        "family": {"type": "string", "enum": ["Kaprekar", "Fibonacci", "RandomMap", "Collatz", "Mandelbrot", "CellularAutomaton", "Custom"]},
        "rule_description": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 500},
        "state_space_size": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1},
        "parameters": {"type": "object"},
        "invariants": {
            "type": "object",
            "required": ["num_cycles", "max_height", "transient_nodes", "quotient_estimate", "compression_ratio"],
            "properties": {
                "num_cycles": {"type": "integer"},
                "cycle_nodes": {"type": "integer"},
                "transient_nodes": {"type": "integer"},
                "max_height": {"type": "integer"},
                "height_spectrum": {"type": "object"},
                "inverse_branching_max": {"type": "integer"},
                "entropy_indegree": {"type": "number"},
                "quotient_estimate": {"type": "integer"},
                "compression_ratio": {"type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 1}
            }
        },
        "certificate": {
            "type": "object",
            "required": ["type", "status", "timestamp", "engine_version"],
            "properties": {
                "type": {"type": "string", "enum": ["computational", "experimental", "theoretical"]},
                "status": {"type": "string", "enum": ["PASS", "FAIL", "PENDING"]},
                "timestamp": {"type": "string", "format": "date-time"},
                "engine_version": {"type": "string"},
                "checksum": {"type": "string"}
            }
        }
    }
}

with open("/mnt/agents/output/AQARION_benchmark_schema.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(benchmark_schema, f, indent=2)

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("SECTION 5 — BENCHMARK LIBRARY SCHEMA")
print("=" * 90)
print(f"\nSchema defines {len(benchmark_schema['properties'])} top-level fields")
print(f"Required invariants: {', '.join(benchmark_schema['properties']['invariants']['required'])}")
print(f"Certificate types: {benchmark_schema['properties']['certificate']['properties']['type']['enum']}")

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SUMMARY
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("ALL DELIVERABLES")
print("=" * 90)
deliverables = [
    ("AQARION_evidence_framework.json", "Five-layer evidence framework"),
    ("AQARION_decision_outcomes.json", "Pre-defined decision outcomes A–E"),
    ("AQARION_research_programs.json", "Six staged research programs"),
    ("AQARION_observatory_architecture.json", "Observatory architecture L1–L6"),
    ("AQARION_benchmark_schema.json", "Benchmark library JSON schema"),
]
